nativeRange.habitat: Shallow subtidal hard substrate and reef rubble zones in moderate-flow, photic coastal waters

decorPreferences: plug or direct attachment to rockwork

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish likely to graze it to bare thallus

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product photo of Branching Hex (green/red form) from Mosaic Macros listing.
Product photo of Branching Hex Purple Form; coloration varies by lighting conditions.

summary: The Branching Hex is a gelatinous red macroalga with a distinctive rubbery, dichotomously branching thallus. It is available in a standard form and a deeper purple color form, and is considered a specialty display species suited to established reef aquariums.
careNotes:
The Branching Hex (Scinaia sp.) belongs to the family Scinaiaceae in the order Nemaliales — a group of red algae (rhodophytes) closely related to Galaxaura but non-calcified and with softer, more gelatinous tissue. The genus is found across cosmopolitan coastal marine waters of the tropical Indo-Pacific, Atlantic, and subtropical regions. Its rubbery, repeatedly forking thallus creates a hexagonal-grid appearance when viewed from the end — the feature behind the trade name. This alga was formerly sold under the name Rhodopeltis sp., which refers to a distinct genus within the same order; the currently accepted identification is Scinaia sp.

Branching Hex is available through specialty macroalgae retailers rather than general aquarium shops and is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Water parameters and care guidance are extrapolated from family-level Nemaliales biology and experience with comparable gelatinous red macroalgae in reef aquariums. Standard reef parameters are generally advised: temperature 23–27 °C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, and pH 8.1–8.4. Growth tends to be slow, as is typical for ornamental red algae in this order.

The purple form tends to appear maroon or deep red under lower-intensity or warmer-spectrum lighting and shifts toward a pale reddish-purple under higher color-temperature lighting. Moderate lighting is generally suggested to preserve coloration without bleaching.

**MELT WARNING:** Like other red macroalgae, Branching Hex will melt at the base if placed on a sandy substrate. It must be attached to rockwork or rubble — using aquarium-safe superglue, fishing line, zip-ties, or by wedging the base securely between rocks. Do not rest it directly on sand.

Branching Hex is generally compatible with snails, hermit crabs, shrimp, and most peaceful reef fish, though aggressive herbivorous fish may graze it to bare thallus and should be avoided.
breedingNotes: In captivity, propagation is typically achieved through fragmentation: breaking off a portion of the thallus and securing it to rockwork or rubble. Sexual reproduction involving alternation of gametophyte and sporophyte generations occurs in the wild for Nemaliales but has not been documented as a meaningful propagation method in hobby aquariums.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Accepted scientific name Scinaia sp. confirmed from this listing (updated from earlier 'Rhodopeltis sp.' misidentification on alternate URL). Primary hobby source.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros (original listing)
additional.notes: Earlier listing using 'Rhodopeltis sp.' trade name; superseded by Scinaia sp. identification. Confirmed rubbery branching morphology and general reef compatibility guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Scinaiaceae
additional.notes: Confirms family Scinaiaceae, order Nemaliales, suborder Galaxaurineae; cosmopolitan marine distribution; ~45 Scinaia species; gelatinous cortical mucilage characteristic of family. Used for taxonomy and native range extrapolation.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
